flashcards.front: A founder objects: 'I don't know LTV precisely, so I can't do CAC/LTV comparisons.' What's wrong with this?
flashcards.back: Refusing to estimate is worse than a rough estimate. Written guesstimates are comparable across channels; unwritten intuitions are comparable to nothing. The book's rule: guesstimate aggressively and write it down — tests and time will progressively replace guesses with measured values.

flashcards.front: Why does move-the-needle math become *more* important as a company grows?
flashcards.back: Because the needle gets bigger. In Phase I, most channels can plausibly hit a small customer target, so any plausible channel deserves a test. In Phase II/III (post-PMF, larger target), many channels produce real but insufficient volume — they'd 'work' in the sense of producing customers, just not enough to materially shift the growth curve. Without the math, you'd invest time in all of them.

scenarios.setup: A founder running a B2B SaaS at 400 paying customers ($25k MRR) wants to reach 1,500 customers in 6 months. He's considering hosting his own annual user conference as a traction channel — estimated cost $120k, estimated attendance 300, estimated conversion 30%.
scenarios.question: Apply move-the-needle math. Should he pursue this?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Run the numbers: 300 attendees × 30% conversion = 90 new customers from the conference. Target net new: ~1,100 customers over 6 months = ~180/month. The conference contributes 90 customers — roughly half of ONE month's target, and it happens once. Plus $120k is an enormous single spend at his stage; even at $1k CAC from the conference, it's 5-10x his typical CAC. Recommendation: skip it as a primary channel. The ceiling math disqualifies it — best-case outcome is 90 customers against an 1,100 target. That said, don't reject the intuition entirely: the event might be worth doing at a smaller scale as a community-building play (50 people, $15k, retention + referral generation from existing customers) or later once he's at 4,000+ customers and the event can reach 1,000+ with relevant conversion economics. At current stage, the math says no; at a later stage, the same tactic might pass the move-the-needle bar.
rubric: Runs the ceiling math explicitly (90 customers from the event)
Compares to the 1,100-customer target and identifies the gap
Flags the cost ($120k = ~5-10x his normal CAC)
Recommends passing at current stage
Preserves the tactic for a later stage where the math would work
Doesn't fall into 'but events have other benefits' without addressing the math

scenarios.setup: A startup at 50k paying customers wants to reach 200k in a year. Their brainstorm produced 19 plausible channel ideas. The founder wants to test all 19 to be thorough.
scenarios.question: Use the move-the-needle calculation to cut the list. What's your approach?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Do a 20-minute back-of-envelope for each channel: audience ceiling × realistic conversion = plausible contribution. Target is 150k net new customers over 12 months = ~12.5k/month. For each channel, ask: could this channel plausibly deliver 12.5k/month at its ceiling? Channels that can't — like 'speaking at a few niche conferences a year' or 'one-off PR features' — get ruled out even if they're interesting. Channels that could (likely: viral loops, SEM at scale, major platform integrations, broad content+SEO) stay in the inner circle for testing. Expected outcome: 19 channels drop to probably 4-6 that pass the move-the-needle bar, and Bullseye Step-3 prioritization cuts those to 3. The math does the heavy lifting before Step 3 even starts. This is exactly the Phase II/III discipline the book prescribes — many channels 'work' but few move the needle at scale.
rubric: Computes the monthly target (12.5k net new)
Prescribes the per-channel ceiling calculation against that target
Names at least 2 channel types that likely fail the math (niche events, one-off PR)
Names at least 2 channel types that likely pass (viral, SEM at scale, platforms)
Ties the filtered list back to Bullseye Step 3 (prioritize to 3)
Identifies this as a Phase II/III stage-specific discipline

scenarios.setup: A founder insists on testing a channel where the move-the-needle math clearly says no: 'The math is imprecise — my conversion estimate could be 10x off. I want to test it for real.'
scenarios.question: Is his critique of the math fair? How should you respond?
scenarios.modelAnswer: Partially fair, and the response should be proportionate. If his conversion estimate is plausibly 10x off and the channel's ceiling currently lands at ~10% of his target, a 10x swing could bring it to ~target — in which case the math isn't ruling it out confidently and a cheap test is warranted. But the test should be designed specifically to measure the conversion rate cheaply: spend ~$250-500, set a pre-defined threshold for the conversion rate that would justify committing, and if the test shows his conversion guess is closer to reality than his optimistic 10x version, the channel is ruled out with real data instead of a guess. That's actually the right use of the test: to resolve an assumption the math can't resolve. What's *not* fair: using 'the math could be wrong' as blanket permission to ignore ceilings. If the gap is 100x, no plausible assumption error closes it. If the gap is within an order of magnitude, a cheap test is exactly the move. So: honor the critique if the math's uncertainty overlaps the target; dismiss it if it doesn't.
rubric: Takes the critique seriously where math uncertainty is large relative to the gap
Prescribes a cheap test designed specifically to resolve the contested assumption
Pre-defines the conversion threshold that would justify committing
Dismisses the critique when the gap is large enough that no plausible error closes it
Names the right role of the test: resolving an assumption math can't resolve
Doesn't fall into either extreme (ignore-the-math or refuse-all-tests)

teachback.prompt: Explain to a teammate how and why to quantify channel decisions. Cover: (1) why CAC and LTV are universal columns, (2) the move-the-needle calculation and how it rules channels out before testing, (3) why the math becomes more important as the company grows, and (4) how to handle estimates that are deeply uncertain.
rubric: Names CAC and LTV as universal and explains why (they apply to every channel)
Articulates the move-the-needle calculation (ceiling × conversion vs. target)
Explains why growing companies need the math more (bigger needle, more channels that 'work but not enough')
States the 'rough beats none' rule for uncertain estimates
Gives a concrete example (their own startup or DuckDuckGo)
Explanation is causal — explains why each part of the math exists
